claims.claim: By default, competitive SDK ecosystems will converge on workload identity/OIDC and make long-lived API keys a legacy compatibility mode.
claims.why_distinctive: It reframes auth strategy as an ecosystem trajectory rather than provider-specific feature checklists.
claims.falsifier: Primary providers formally reassert API-key-first production guidance and remove federation pathways from default docs/tooling.
claims.verify: Track top-level auth guidance in official SDK docs/release notes across four quarters and classify default examples.

human_input.note: Treat this as directionally strong but rollout-speed variable; multi-cloud operational maturity will determine adoption pace more than SDK capability alone.

claims.claim: In SDK-heavy systems, release governance discipline now contributes more to reliability than adding bespoke abstraction layers over SDKs.
claims.why_distinctive: It shifts reliability investment from code abstraction effort to version-policy and rollout controls.
claims.falsifier: Organizations with weak release governance but heavy custom wrappers consistently outperform teams with strong governance and minimal wrappers.
claims.verify: Compare incident and rollback rates across teams with different governance-vs-wrapper investment profiles.

human_input.note: Wrappers still matter for domain boundaries, but they should not substitute for dependency governance, changelog triage, and controlled rollout lanes.

claims.claim: The winning developer teams in 2026 will be those that formalize framework runtime directives as policy artifacts, not those that rely on tribal memory and ad-hoc review.
claims.why_distinctive: It treats runtime directives as governance primitives and predicts execution advantage from policy codification.
claims.falsifier: High-change teams without formal runtime-policy codification maintain equal defect and delivery outcomes over multiple release cycles.
claims.verify: Measure boundary-defect density and lead time across teams before and after codifying runtime-directive policy gates.

human_input.note: Apply this first to high-blast-radius surfaces (cache invalidation, hydration, webhook handling) before broad policy expansion.
